Salk Institute for Biological Studies · Laboratory of Genetics

Neuronal Plasticity
& Genomic Diversity

The Gage Laboratory explores the extraordinary adaptability of the adult brain — from the discovery that new neurons grow throughout life, to unlocking the secrets of aging, memory, and neurological disease.

Explore Discoveries Join the Lab

"The brain retains a remarkable capacity for change — and that changes everything we thought we knew about neurological disease."
Principal Investigator

Fred H. "Rusty" Gage

Professor · Vi and John Adler Chair · Laboratory of Genetics

Dr. Fred H. "Rusty" Gage is one of the world's foremost neuroscientists. His landmark 1998 discovery that the adult human brain generates new neurons — a process called neurogenesis — fundamentally overturned decades of scientific consensus and opened entirely new frontiers in brain biology.

His laboratory at the Salk Institute concentrates on the adult central nervous system and the unexpected plasticity and adaptability to environmental stimulation that remains throughout the life of all mammals. His work ranges from single-cell genomics to patient-derived iPSC models of Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and schizophrenia.

Dr. Gage is a past President of the Society for Neuroscience, a member of the National Academy of Sciences and the National Academy of Medicine, and has received over three dozen major awards for his contributions to science and medicine.

Adult Neurogenesis Cellular Reprogramming Alzheimer's Disease iPSC Modeling LINE-1 Retrotransposons Neuronal Mosaicism Human Evolution Memory & Learning
Scientific Legacy

Four Decades of Discovery

A scroll through the breakthroughs that shaped modern neuroscience — from the Gage Lab to the world.

1983

Neural Transplantation in Aged Rats

Early work demonstrating that fetal brain tissue transplants could restore cognitive function in aged rodents — a first hint that the aging brain is not immutably fixed, and that environmental intervention matters.

Neuroplasticity
1994

Exercise Enhances Brain Cell Growth

Demonstrating that physical exercise dramatically increases neurogenesis in the adult hippocampus — one of the first proofs that lifestyle factors can physically reshape the brain. Foundational for understanding cognitive decline prevention.

View Publication Neurogenesis · Exercise
1998

🏆 Landmark: Neurogenesis in the Adult Human Brain

Published in Nature Medicine with Eriksson et al., this study provided the first direct evidence that the human brain continues to generate new neurons throughout adulthood — overturning a century of scientific dogma that "no new neurons are born after childhood."

Eriksson et al. · Nature Medicine Landmark Discovery · Nature Medicine
2001

Environmental Enrichment & Neurogenesis

Showing that environmental complexity — not just exercise — boosts new neuron survival in the hippocampus. Established that the external world shapes the internal biology of the brain throughout life, with major implications for aging and Alzheimer's prevention.

Hippocampus · Learning
2009

LINE-1 Retrotransposon Activity in Neurons

Discovery that LINE-1 (L1) mobile elements actively retrotranspose during neurogenesis, creating unique genomic diversity among individual neurons in a single brain. This somatic mosaicism concept transformed our understanding of neuronal individuality and disease risk.

Muotri et al. · Nature Genomics · LINE-1 · Mosaicism
2012

iPSC Disease Modeling: Schizophrenia & ASD

Pioneering use of patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells to generate neurons from individuals with schizophrenia, revealing disease-specific connectivity deficits that could not be studied any other way. Opened the era of "disease-in-a-dish" neuroscience.

Brennand et al. · Nature iPSC · Schizophrenia
2015

Single-Cell Genomic Diversity in Human Neurons

Using single-cell genome sequencing to reveal the extraordinary degree of copy number variation among individual neurons in healthy human brains — establishing that each neuron carries a unique genome, with implications for aging, cancer, and neurodegeneration.

Single-Cell · Copy Number Variation
2018

Direct Reprogramming Preserves Aging Signatures

Demonstrating that neurons derived by direct conversion from aged fibroblasts — bypassing iPSC reprogramming — retain donor epigenetic aging signatures, making them far superior models for late-onset diseases like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's.

Mertens et al. · Cell Stem Cell Direct Conversion · Aging · Alzheimer's
2022

Human–Chimpanzee Neural Differences via iPSCs

Using reprogramming to generate neurons from chimpanzee and human iPSCs side-by-side, revealing functional and genomic differences that could underlie the unique cognitive capacities of Homo sapiens — a living window into human evolution.

Human Evolution · NHP
2024+

Astrocyte Direct Conversion & Aging Models

Ongoing work developing CRISPRa-based protocols to directly convert aged human fibroblasts into induced astrocytes (iAs) that retain donor aging signatures — a new platform for understanding glial contributions to late-onset neurological disease.

CRISPRa · Astrocytes · Aging
Current Focus

Research Areas

Four interconnected pillars of discovery that span molecular biology, human evolution, and clinical translation.

01
🧠

Neurogenesis

The continuous incorporation of new neurons into the dentate gyrus raises exciting questions about brain plasticity during memory acquisition and learning in adulthood. We use live imaging of active neurons and single cell sequencing during memory acquisition to understand the molecular basis of memory encoding.

HippocampusMemoryLive ImagingSingle-Cell
02
🔬

Modeling Neurological Disorders with iPSCs & iN

Patient-derived iPSCs and directly reprogrammed neurons allow us to capture the genetic architecture of patients with schizophrenia, ASD, bipolar disorder, Parkinson's, and MS — comparing neuronal connectivity, firing properties, and gene expression to identify disease phenotypes.

iPSCDirect ReprogrammingParkinson'sSchizophrenia
03
🔀

Neuronal Mosaicism & Genomic Diversity

Mobile elements such as LINE-1 (L1) and Alu retrotransposons actively retrotranspose during neurogenesis, creating neuron-to-neuron genomic diversity. We use single-cell genome isolation and next-generation sequencing to reveal somatic DNA insertions and their consequences for brain function and disease.

LINE-1AluSomatic MosaicismNGS
04
🦍

Human Evolution

Using reprogramming technology to generate iPSCs and neurons from chimpanzees, bonobos, and gorillas alongside human cells, we elucidate the phenotypical differences between humans and non-human primates that have potential adaptation value and contribute to our understanding of great ape evolution.

Non-Human PrimatesiPSCEvolution

Press & Updates

News & Highlights

Recent developments from the Gage Lab and the Salk Institute.

Lab photo placeholder

2024

Astrocyte Direct Conversion Platform Advances Aging Research

New CRISPRa-based protocols enable conversion of aged fibroblasts into induced astrocytes that preserve donor aging signatures, creating superior models for late-onset neurological disease.

Read on Salk Press
Lab photo placeholder

2023

Single-Cell Analysis Reveals New Mechanism of Memory Formation

Gage Lab research using live calcium imaging and single-cell sequencing identifies specific neural signatures of new memory encoding in adult-born hippocampal neurons.

Read on Salk Press
Lab photo placeholder

2022

Human–Chimpanzee iPSC Study Illuminates Cognitive Evolution

By generating and comparing neurons from human and non-human primate iPSCs, the Gage Lab identifies molecular signatures that may underlie uniquely human cognitive abilities.

Read on Salk Press
